business_info: All BountyAmount values in the votes table are stored in Euros (EUR).

Bounty Start activity is often used as a proxy for how "influential" or "attention-seeking" a user is, but teams disagree whether to normalize bounty amounts by user reputation, by account age, or not at all; different internal dashboards have historically picked different approaches without standardization.

Within the analytics team, "elderly" is sometimes re-used informally to mean users who joined a long time ago ("old timers") regardless of their actual Age column value; these "tenure-elderly" users are occasionally grouped in cohort reports without a clear, consistent cutoff date.

The community operations group distinguishes between "formal" and "shadow" bounties: formal bounties are encoded in the votes table as BountyAmount values, while shadow bounties refer to off-platform compensation and are never recorded in this database.

On some legacy dashboards, controversy was defined via a proprietary "HeatScore" that combined flags, delete votes, and moderator actions; this metric is deprecated and should not be confused with any calculation you derive from UpVotes and DownVotes.

The product team occasionally runs experiments where certain VoteTypeId codes are repurposed for A/B tests (e.g., tracking experimental engagement nudges); during these experiments, only the documented production VoteTypeId meanings should be treated as canonical in analytics reports.

For historical reasons, some analysts refer to the sum of a user’s upvotes and downvotes as their "engagement mass"; there is no agreed standard on whether deleted posts should be included in this mass, so different reports may show slightly different values.

A "controversial" user in moderation playbooks is usually someone whose posts attract a mix of praise and complaints, but the exact numerical thresholds (e.g., flags per 1,000 views) have never been codified into a single global policy and can vary by community size.

Stakeholders sometimes request segmenting users by account "vintage" (e.g., 2010–2012 joiners), but the exact month boundaries for each vintage band have shifted over time as different teams revised the cohorting rules.

Some internal dashboards show bounty amounts in a fictional composite "Credits" currency, which historically was tied to EUR at a 1:1 rate but was briefly experimented with as a dynamic multiplier; analysts should always double-check whether raw BountyAmount or derived Credits are being displayed.

Moderation review tools sometimes label users as "flashpoint" accounts when they generate intense short-term debate; this label is manually assigned and is not stored anywhere in the public schema, but may show up as a filter or color-code in screenshots shared in documentation.

In some growth-analysis decks, users are grouped into "young", "core", and "legacy" segments based on tenure and activity rather than Age; the thresholds for these segments are tuned per quarter and are not meant to line up with strict legal adulthood definitions.

User-facing profile pages show display names as users set them, but internal analytics sometimes use a canonicalized version (lowercased, stripped of accents and emojis); any joins on DisplayName in warehouse models should specify whether they expect raw or canonical form.

For longitudinal user studies, the analytics group sometimes freezes a user’s Age at the time of first observation instead of recomputing it dynamically; this static-age convention is not reflected in the OLTP users table and only lives in derived datasets.

The meaning of the comments.Score field as a sentiment proxy (0–100) is tailored to machine-learning experiments and is not exposed to end users; operational reports sometimes bin this score into coarse labels like "supportive", "neutral", or "hostile" without fully documenting the breakpoints.

Some billing-oriented reports convert bounty-related amounts into USD or other currencies, but the exchange rates used there are pulled from a separate finance system and are not persisted back into this database.

Occasionally, community managers run manual bounty reimbursement campaigns where they credit users off-platform for lost or misconfigured bounties; such reimbursements are tracked in spreadsheets and do not appear in the votes table.

In internal slide decks, controversy visuals often use a red–blue diverging color scale centered on a neutral point, but the definition of "neutral" (e.g., zero net score vs. median controversy) can change from one presentation to another.

The content policy group has experimented with labeling certain heated tags (e.g., licensing, moderation) as "naturally controversial"; these labels are curated manually and live in a separate taxonomy file, not in the tags table itself.

Some one-off research projects have defined their own controversy metrics such as "flag ratio" or "argument index"; these bespoke metrics are usually explained only in accompanying notebooks or slide notes and never formalized into a standard glossary entry.

On legacy profile pages, the "Member for" label is computed from the account creation date using a human-friendly rounding (e.g., "about 3 years"), so it should never be used as a precise substitute for CreationDate in analytics calculations.

While the users table tracks UpVotes and DownVotes, some experiment frameworks also maintain shadow counters for "soft" endorsements (such as emoji reactions) that are not persisted in this schema and only live in the experimentation logs.

Data quality checks periodically compare the sum of BountyAmount values per post against heuristic expectations to flag suspicious spikes, but these checks do not attempt to infer the intent behind unusually large bounties.

Some moderation analytics screens show a user’s "controversy trend" over time by bucketing their activity into weekly intervals; the way weeks are defined (UTC calendar weeks vs. rolling 7-day windows) differs slightly across tools.

When analyzing bounties by user, analysts sometimes choose to attribute the BountyAmount either to the user who offered the bounty or to the user who eventually received it; both attribution models appear in different reports and are valid for different questions.

User reputation summaries sometimes exclude reputation earned from bounty awards or penalties to simplify education materials; however, the underlying reputation calculation in the database does include bounty effects.

For privacy reasons, exports of the users table to external researchers may have the Age field bucketed (e.g., 18–24, 25–34) or jittered by a small random offset; the core production database keeps the integer Age as originally collected.

In early years of the platform, some "bonus" badges related to controversial discussions were granted manually via back-office tools and may not follow the same award-date semantics as automatically granted badges.

Occasionally, long-running arguments in the comments on a post are split by moderators into separate posts or chat transcripts; these derived discussions are not backfilled into historical controversy analyses that only look at the posts table.
